skill.name: reverse-engineer
skill.description: Guides authorized reverse engineering of binaries, libraries, and protocols using structured static and dynamic analysis workflows.
skill.summary: Plan and document authorized binary reverse engineering with clear analysis steps.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: The seven static Ruby or shell backtick findings are false positives from Markdown fences, inline documentation, and example command text. The weak cryptographic algorithm finding at SKILL.md:3 is also a false positive because that line is descriptive metadata. The skill is publishable with a warning because reverse engineering and vulnerability research are dual-use activities, even though the file includes authorization and ethics limits.

medium_findings.title: Dual-Use Reverse Engineering Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill covers binary analysis, vulnerability research, dynamic analysis, and closed-source authentication review. These are legitimate security tasks, but they can be misused without authorization. The skill includes explicit authorized-use limits and prohibited-use guidance, so this is a medium-risk marketplace warning rather than a block.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill purpose and examples directly reference reverse engineering, vulnerability research, and authentication analysis. The ethics section reduces risk but does not remove the dual-use nature.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks Flagged as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged Ruby or shell backtick execution at several lines. These locations are Markdown code fences, inline documentation, or example interaction formatting, not executable skill code. No automatic command execution path was found in the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are visible Markdown syntax or documentation text in SKILL.md. There is no script file or runtime mechanism that executes these backticks.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Detection in Metadata
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged a weak cryptographic algorithm at SKILL.md:3. That line is part of the YAML description and says the skill is for expert reverse engineering. It does not name or implement a cryptographic algorithm.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited line contains descriptive metadata only. No cryptographic code or algorithm selection appears at that location.

low_findings.title: User-Mediated External Tool Examples
low_findings.description: The skill suggests tools such as file, checksec, GDB, dumpbin, Frida, and network monitoring in examples. These are normal reverse-engineering tools, but users should run them only on authorized samples and isolated systems.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples clearly show command-line and analysis-tool usage. They are instructions for the user, not commands executed by the skill.

dangerous_patterns.title: External Command Guidance in Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes example command-line analysis steps for CTF binaries and library inspection. This is expected for reverse engineering, but it creates operational risk if used on untrusted files outside isolation.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands and tool names are explicit in the examples. The surrounding text frames them as manual analysis steps rather than automated execution.

content.user_title: Analyze Binaries With Reverse Engineering Workflows
content.value_statement: Binary analysis can be hard to structure and document safely. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a clear workflow for authorized reverse engineering tasks.
actual_capabilities: Plans static and dynamic analysis phases for binaries and libraries.
Recommends common tools such as IDA Pro, Ghidra, radare2, GDB, and Frida.
Explains common code patterns, calling conventions, and obfuscation signals.
Helps document functions, data structures, algorithms, and analysis findings.
Frames work around authorized research, CTF tasks, and defensive malware analysis.
limitations: It does not perform automated disassembly or decompilation by itself.
It requires the user to provide binaries, tool output, or analysis observations.
It should not be used for unauthorized access, malware creation, or license bypass.
Complex packed or obfuscated samples may still require expert manual review.

use_cases.title: CTF Binary Triage
use_cases.description: Plan a step-by-step approach for identifying a challenge binary, finding key checks, and documenting the solution path.

use_cases.title: Defensive Malware Review
use_cases.description: Organize safe static and dynamic analysis of a suspicious sample in an isolated environment.

use_cases.title: Library Behavior Mapping
use_cases.description: Document exported functions, authentication paths, data structures, and observable behavior in an authorized library review.

prompt_templates.title: Start Binary Triage
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner triage of an authorized sample.
prompt_templates.prompt: I have authorization to analyze this binary. Help me plan initial triage, including file type, architecture, imports, strings, and safe next steps.

prompt_templates.title: Interpret Tool Output
prompt_templates.scenario: Review results from common reverse-engineering tools.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Ghidra, IDA, radare2, or GDB output. Explain likely functions, control flow, important strings, and evidence-backed next steps.

prompt_templates.title: Map Suspicious Behavior
prompt_templates.scenario: Analyze suspicious or defensive malware-analysis findings.
prompt_templates.prompt: For this authorized sample, help me map observed behaviors, API calls, persistence signals, network indicators, and safe validation steps.

prompt_templates.title: Document Full Findings
prompt_templates.prompt: Turn my reverse-engineering notes into a structured report with scope, methodology, function summaries, data structures, risks, and reproducible evidence.

output_examples.input: User provides a CTF binary description and asks where to begin.
output_examples.output: A triage plan covering file identification, protections, strings, imports, main function discovery, comparison points, and evidence to record.

output_examples.input: User provides notes from an authorized DLL authentication review.
output_examples.output: A structured analysis path covering exports, likely authentication functions, dynamic hooks, parameter tracing, and documentation checkpoints.

output_examples.input: User provides observations from a suspicious sample in a lab VM.
output_examples.output: A defensive analysis summary that separates confirmed behavior, indicators to validate, sandbox precautions, and follow-up questions.

best_practices: Confirm authorization and scope before analyzing software or sharing findings.
Use isolated environments for unknown binaries and record tool versions and evidence.
Separate observed facts from hypotheses when documenting reverse-engineering results.
anti_patterns: Do not use the skill to bypass licensing, steal intellectual property, or gain unauthorized access.
Do not run unknown binaries on a personal or production system.
Do not present guesses as confirmed behavior without reproducible evidence.

faq.question: Does this skill execute reverse-engineering tools automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and analysis structure. Users run approved tools in their own controlled environment.

faq.question: Can it help with CTF challenges?
faq.answer: Yes. It can help plan triage, identify likely checks, interpret tool output, and document a solution path.

faq.question: Can it analyze malware?
faq.answer: It can support defensive malware analysis when the user works in an isolated lab and has authorization.

faq.question: What tools does it reference?
faq.answer: It references IDA Pro, Ghidra, radare2, x64dbg, GDB, Frida, r2pipe, angr, capstone, and related tooling.

faq.question: Is this suitable for proprietary software review?
faq.answer: Only when the user has authorization, a legal right to inspect the software, or a valid interoperability purpose.

faq.question: What should users provide for best results?
faq.answer: Provide scope, authorization context, file metadata, tool output, observed behavior, and specific questions to answer.
